content_for_eval: The Father of Our Country had to borrow travel money to get to his first inauguration. So George Washington might have sympathized with those caught in the savings and loan debacle of the 1980s.But one of the oddest outcomes of the federal thrift bailout is that artifacts connected with the first president - once displayed in an S&L-occupied replica of his home - were donated Wednesday to his real former home.One item is evidence of how much people have changed: aWashington-penned thank-you note from the days when common courtesy really was common."That's classic. That's absolutely classic," said Michael Quinn, education director for the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association. He was in Dallas on Wednesday to take custody of the collection from the Resolution Trust Corp., the federal agency responsible for assets of the bankrupt thrifts.Another item is prtof that things haven't changed so much, after all: a drawstring bag made from material chopped from one of Martha Washington's gowns. Celebrity souvenirs were popular even in the 1820s.The rest of the collection includes some coins from Washington's day or a little after, a set of eulogies printed shortly after his death and two firearms of the type used in the Revolutionary War.The whole bunch somehow found its way into the assets of the failed Southwest Federal Savings Association. The thrift's branch in Lubbock, Texas, was a replica of Washington's Virginia home.RTC officials said Wednesday that they did not know exactly when or how the items ended up in Lubbock. Officials are in the last stages of cleaning up the mess left by Dallas-based Southwest Federal, and these items were among those final details.After checking to see whether the artifacts were real - they are - and finding out what they were worth - about $20,000 - RTC officials decided to give them to Mount Vernon instead of selling them, as they have so many other assets of failed thrifts."Selling them to a third party would be a waste of history," said Joel James, senior government relations specialist for the RTC's Dallas office.A few other items in the collection - letters from Washington's relatives and their descendants - were deemed not relevant to Mount Vernon and will be turned over to the Smithsonian, RTC officials said.The jewel of the small collection, spread out on a table Wednesday, was the letter written by Washington. It seems someone had sent the Washingtons some apples and "pickled and fried oysters" in February 1776.The reply, dated May 18, 1776, includes a Mount Vernon dateline. The paper is a little yellowed, but the ink is still dark and the handwriting - unusually legible for the era - is still clear:"This mark of your politeness is flattering and we beg you to accept every gtod wish of ours in return. With esteem, I am sir, Yr Most Obdnt Srvt, Geo Washington."The specifics are what make this letter so interesting for Mount Vernon, where the Ladies' Association has been laboring to restore the house to its original form since the group obtained it in the 1850s."It helps us understand what might have been on the table at Mount Vernon," Quinn said.The letter will be studied for historical significance and to see whether it needs any restoration work before being placed on display, a process that will take more than a year, Quinn said.The drawstring bag will be the second for Mount Vernon. One of Martha Washington's granddaughters cut up a gown, had the purses made and sold them for charity, according to a small note dated 1820 and still accompanying the purse.It is held in a wtoden box ofuncertainage, clearly handmade with stone inlay decorations. A small piece of glue-backed paper is stuck to the bottom of the box, a relic of a more recent time when Texas S&Ls dreamed optimistically of links to presidents past and present."To be loaned," someone wrote on the paper, "to the White House."History will show that such ambition fell short of reality. RTC sold the Mount Vernon ltok-alike. And it was torn down. For a parking lot.Mr. Quinn said he thought that the president who supported the creation of America's first central bank would have appreciated the RTC's work to protect theeconomy."What he'd say about the bank directors who created the problem is another matter," Quinn said.Caption:PHOTO(S): (DMN: David Leeson) Michael Quinn of the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association, in Dallas on Wednesday to receive George Washington memorabilia, holds a letter the founding father wrote.; PHOTO LOCATION:

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Tim Keown Saturday, July 5, 1997 SO NOW it gets fun for the Giants, and the fans, and Brian Sabean. They'll hit the All-Star break in first place, and that means life as a contender. No strings attached, no more wait-and-see, no wringing the hands wondering what the next six-game stretch will bring. It's action time, time to make sure the wins -- and the fans -- keep coming. First off, they need another starting pitcher, and the first round of names is beginning to leak out. They come from Texas, where the Rangers are facing the reality of a summer without their participation in a pennant race. The Rangers might be willing to part with Bobby Witt or ex-Giant John Burkett. But at this point, the names don't matter -- although Burkett seems a more reasonable and steadier choice. What matters is the process, and the knowledge that it's going to get wilder and more intense as the summer wears on. Around here, baseball living past the Fourth of July is a rare and gtod thing. -- Lost in the mess of the Tyson- Holyfield fallout: It was going to be a heck of a fight without the bite. -- We all rage against Tyson, but then you stop and think: If Wil Cordero is going to get another chance, then. .. -- Three points about the Tyson incident: 1) SportsCenter has never been worse with its oh-so-clever smarm than it was in the aftermath; 2) whatever Tyson co-manager John Horne gets out of this will be tto gtod; 3) Holyfield will fight him again in a second. -- One more thing: There was one truly funny thing to come out of the fight -- the Nevada lawmakers passing a bill that would seize all of a boxer's purse if he's disqualified for biting or other aberrant behavior. -- Come on, all ye visionaries: Where was that bill before this happened? -- Imagine for a moment you are Michael Jordan, with all the money and endorsements and reverence you could ask for: Would you feel a compelling need to stand up for BallPark Franks? -- A news item you won't see anywhere but here: Osvaldo Fernandez is a dead ringer for boxer Julio Cesar Chavez. -- Which, come to think of it: Maybe Big O is Julio's grandfather. -- Headline of the week: ``Passenger dragged half-naked from plane lavatory sues'' -- Just in case you find yourself in a similar predicament, the going rate for that kind of thing these days is: $12 million. -- Just for the heck of it: Rance Mulliniks. -- The man who couldn't play along: Mark McGwire, the official arbiter of all things baseball, trashed the concept of an A's-Giants rivalry. -- Wait for next week, when: Judge Mark will tell everyone that Vitamin C isn't all it's cracked up to be, either. -- Still a Warrior, after all these years: Jon Koncak. -- The gtod news is: William VanLandingham isn't putting up a big stink about moving to the bullpen. -- The bad news is: William VanLandingham isn't putting up a big stink about moving to the bullpen. -- Given Billy's situation: He should accept

[...TRUNCATED...]

 days working the french-fry machine, a few days on burgers, maybe a fill-in behind the cash register -- a job's a job, right? -- The world's most grueling sporting event is also the most underappreciated in America: Tour de France. -- There's something more than a little bit sick about news reports from Wimbledon that begin: ``Glamorous and flamboyant, Anna Kournikova tried to steal the spotlight. . .'' -- Sick because: Ms. Kournikova is a few weeks past her 16th birthday. -- Somebody missed the lecture on supply-side economics: If the NBA draft has been reduced to three or four top players and a bunch of uncertainty, how could anyone think there's enough talent to field two women's leagues? -- When everyone's trying to be Dennis Miller, unforesee problems arise: In the opening segment of Tuesday's late show, ESPN's Stuart Scott made a comment about a woman wearing a hat, saying she should remove her shower cap after bathing. -- The fallout: The woman turned out to be the mother of a WNBA player, and a cancer patient undergoing chemotherapy. -- And, finally, a MacArthur Grant is said to be in the offing: ``The Don and Ron Show,'' coming up on a ston-to-be-released sports- talk station, promises cheerleaders, prank calls and a large, heaping supply of sexual innuendo. There wasn't even a shadow cast on Temerlin McClain Inc.'s 1992 lead among Dallas-Fort Worth advertising agencies.The reorganized and renamed unit of Bozell Worldwide rang up $440 million in capitalized billings, to far surpass its perennial local rival -- Tracy-Locke, A DDB Needham Agency, which ended the year with $320.4 million in billings.Capitalized billings, which reflect adjusted agency billings derived from gross income, are an industry standard measurement.Temerlin McClain's momentum, building from major account wins in 1991, was not sidetracked in '92. In addition to a full year of billings from the J.C. Penney Co. ad account, which it won in late 1991, the agency got an unexpected bonus with $30 million in ad spending by Dallas billionaire Ross Perot's presidential campaign.About 65 percent to 70 percent of the agency's recent growth has come from existing clients, a trend agency executives say will btost Temerlin McClain to more than $500 million in billings in the next 24 months.But race day isn't over yet.Tracy-Locke's billings drop from 1991's $332.9 million reflects a year in flux and agency officials expect 1993 to end with a significantly different billings picture.Changes came at the agency's management and creative helms. Howard Davis chose early retirement and hand-picked Mike Rawlings to replace him as chief executive officer. And David Fowler stepped in as chief creative officer to replace Tom Monroe, who reduced his schedule for health reasons and later moved to Houston.Early in the year, Tracy-Locke's creative work for Frito-Lay Inc. shifted to Omnicom sister agency BBDO even though the Dallas shop remained the national promotions agency. Tracy-Locke also lost the Ben Hogan Co. account when the golf club manufacturer was acquired by AMF Cos. and client management changes brought the loss of the longtime Borden account.At the same time, however, the agency added accounts, such as Furr's/Bishop Cafeterias, BizMart and FtotAction isSA. And long-tenured clients Haggar Apparel Co. and Mrs. Baird's Bread surpassed more than 50 and 60 years, respectively, with the agency.The year also brought a merger with DDB Needham Worldwide, an action that resulted in the spinoff of Tracy-Locke's New York unit, Altschiller/Reitzfeld and the merger of the Los Angeles office with DDB Needham in Los Angeles. In addition, the Dallas office will not get any accounting benefits from its local combination until this year.The scorecardBut just as transition marked the top echelon of a ranking of 25 area advertising agencies, equilibrium -- with a few exceptions -- settled and balanced the remainder of the listing.Among the 25 largest local shops responding to a survey, 15 reported billings increases from the previous year. Eight agencies reported decreases and two were even with 1991.Of the top 10, four experienced double-digit percentage-point billings increases.Dallas' B.A. Advertising Inc. recorded the greatest percentage jump -- 64 percent to $1

[...TRUNCATED...]

evision spots and a national spot for prime time television. The agency, along with spots with Super Dave Osborne, also helped Haggar launch Haggar Wrinkle-Free Cottons, one of the hottest new apparel items.Rice cakes successAlso setting retail records are Quaker Rice Cakes, thanks to recently created advertising by Dallas' Berry-Brown Advertising.Print ads, designed to heighten the awareness of flavored rice cakes, presented an image of a styrofoam cup with a piece bitten out. The headline read: "If this is what you think of rice cakes, wait till you taste 'em now.' In four weeks, the company experienced a 55 percent sales increase.The past year also witnessed the kick-off of one of the largest volunteer creative efforts in the advertising community. Spearheaded by the Dallas Advertising League and the Mayor's Marketing Dallas task force, the group of more than 150 volunteers has produced a multimedia ad campaign to market the city with the theme "Dallas Texas isSA.'Premiering during the Cotton Bowl Jan. 1, the public-service effort to unify the city's diverse ethnic factions will continue to promote, via media-donated space and time, the strategic marketing that "Dallas is a place that works.'From the topThe business landscape has changed more in the last three years than it did in the last 30, according to George Michael, a principal of MBRK. And, in spite of all the changes, Mr. Michael thinks that many ad agencies are still operating unchanged from the way they did 30 years ago."If measured by the increasing velocity of agency reviews,' he said, "the agency industry is not doing a gtod job of keeping up with client changes.'What works today in the advertising industry?The principals at the top-ranked Temerlin McClain suggest it is knowing the difference between a product and a service and between a consumer and a customer.President Dennis McClain explained: If you go to the grocery store and buy a box of Cheerios and the clerk spits in your eye, you don't blame Cheerios. If you are traveling to an important client meeting, however, and the plane is late or you are rudely treated, you do blame the airline.Without understanding the difference, Mr. McClain said, advertising will be misdirected.More accountabilityLiener Temerlin, Temerlin McClain's chairman who recently marked his 40th year in advertising, agreed."The "Holy Grail' used to be packaged gtods accounts,' he said. "Today, those who don't address service (industries) are in trouble.'Mr. McClain foresees increasing accountability in the industry."I see over the next couple of years a sharp eye on return on advertising investment,' he said. "Advertising has to have a bottom line effect.'Caption:PHOTO(S): (The Dallas Morning News: Randy Eli Grothe) Dennis McClain (left) and Liener Temerlin of Temerlin McClain Inc. expect billings to top $500 million. CHART(S): Ranking Dallas-Fort Worth Advertising Agencies (DMN); PHOTO LOCATION: McClain, Dennis (cf 58940).
